harry_takes: OpenAI's internal drama is showing; it has an air of desperation that makes people not want to use their products
Anthropic is clear and consistent; OpenAI gets whiplash from conflicting product directions
Claude is establishing quality lock-in in coding and vibe coding; once you dial in agents, switching models is prohibitively expensive
Token costs matter only up to a certain percentage of revenue; when apps are 5-10% of revenue on tokens, optimizing down is pointless
harry_boldest_quote: I don't want to try their new products because of it, honestly.
predictions: Lemkin: RAMP data is accurate—Anthropic is winning new enterprise spending decisively
Both: If Claude dominates coding/enterprise for another 6-12 months, OpenAI sacrifices lifetime value it cannot recover
O'Driscoll: Enterprises will not switch models once dialed in, regardless of token cost savings, creating durable moat
disagreements: Harry defends OpenAI's optionality and suggests refocus on consumer + enterprise coding could work; Jason and Rory argue lock-in effects mean delays are already damaging and unlikely recoverable.
harry_role: Defending OpenAI against full bearishness. Argues they still own consumer. Cautions against overreading RAMP data (Cursor debate parallel—maybe only VC portfolio sees Claude). Pragmatist.
notable: Lock-in of coding applications to Claude models occurs not from cost but from high soft costs of switching and retraining once dialed in—undermines commoditization thesis.
